mnemonics.title: De Morgan's Law Mantra
mnemonics.content: Break the bar, change the operator: NOT(A·B) = NOT A + NOT B. The overline (bar) acts like parentheses — break it, then flip the AND to OR and vice versa.

mnemonics.title: AND-OR-NOT Gate Recall
mnemonics.content: AND looks like a D shape (Door), OR looks like a curved shield, NOT is a triangle with a bubble (bubble means inversion/negation).

mnemonics.title: Boolean Laws Song
mnemonics.content: A + 0 = A, A + 1 = 1 (ONE for OR wins), A·0 = 0 (ZERO for AND kills), A·1 = A. Complement flips: A + A' = 1, A·A' = 0.

mnemonics.title: K-Map Grouping Rule
mnemonics.content: Power of 2s only: groups of 1, 2, 4, 8, 16... Wrap around edges allowed. Larger groups = simpler terms. Every cell must be covered.

mnemonics.title: XOR vs XNOR Finger Check
mnemonics.content: XOR = 1 when inputs are DIFFERENT (like cross fingers for 'different'). XNOR = 1 when inputs are SAME (like parallel fingers for 'same').

mnemonics.title: Sum of Products (SOP) vs Product of Sums (POS)
mnemonics.content: SOP = OR of ANDs (think: Sum = OR, Product = AND). POS = AND of ORs. Use SOP for minterms (1s in truth table), POS for maxterms (0s).

mnemonics.title: 7-Segment Display Logic
mnemonics.content: Light segments: a (top), b (top-right), c (bottom-right), d (bottom), e (bottom-left), f (top-left), g (middle). Light up specific segments to form digits 0-9.

mnemonics.title: Flip-Flop Truth Table Trick
mnemonics.content: J-K Flip-Flop: J=K=0 = No Change (memory). J=K=1 = Toggle (flip). J=1,K=0 = Set. J=0,K=1 = Reset.

acronyms.usage: Use when simplifying from truth table minterms (1s). Write each 1 as an AND term, then OR all terms together.
Use when simplifying from truth table maxterms (0s). Write each 0 as an OR term, then AND all terms together.
Visual method to simplify Boolean expressions. Group adjacent 1s (for SOP) or 0s (for POS) in powers of 2.
Data selector - selects one input from many based on select lines. 2^n inputs require n select lines.
Data distributor - routes one input to many outputs based on select lines. Opposite of MUX.
Output is 1 when inputs differ. Useful for parity checking, addition (half adder), and controlled inversion.
Output is 1 when inputs are same (equals). Called equivalence gate. Used in comparison circuits.
IC category with 10-100 gates per chip (e.g., adders, decoders, multiplexers).
Standard digital IC family using bipolar junction transistors. Uses 5V supply, known by 7400 series.
Low-power logic family using MOSFETs. Powers most modern digital devices.

visualTips.visualization: AND gate = curved shield shape (like a D). OR gate = pointed shield with curved back. NOT gate = triangle with circle at tip. NAND/NOR = AND/OR with bubble at output. XOR = OR with extra curved line.
Imagine a wall (overline/bar) being broken. When you break the wall, the operations inside flip: AND becomes OR, OR becomes AND. The inverted inputs also flip (A becomes A').
Think of a grid where adjacent cells differ by only one bit (Gray code). Groups wrap around edges. Circle 1s in squares of 2, 4, 8... Each group eliminates changing variables.
List all input combinations giving output=1. For each 1, write an AND term with each input as-is (if 1) or inverted (if 0). OR all these AND terms together.
Two inputs (A, B). Sum = A XOR B (different bits give 1). Carry = A AND B (both 1s give carry).
Three inputs (A, B, Carry-in). Sum = A XOR B XOR Cin. Carry-out = (A AND B) OR (Cin AND (A XOR B)).
Think of a rotary switch. Select lines choose which data input connects to output. 2^n data lines need n select lines to pick one.
Think of a light bulb. Q = current state (bulb on/off). Q' = complement (other state). Clock edge triggers state change based on inputs.

analogies.analogy: Like a series circuit with two switches — current flows (output = 1) only if BOTH switches are closed.
Like parallel switches — current flows if ANY switch is closed (at least one path exists).
Like a normally-open relay that closes when energized — output is opposite of input. Like a NO (normally open) switch.
Like a combination lock that opens unless ALL correct numbers are entered — it inverts the AND result.
Like a light controlled by two-way switches — light turns on if switches are in DIFFERENT positions.
Like a railway signal point — one input track is directed to one of several output tracks based on control signals.
Like a store shelf with Set and Reset buttons. Set = place item on shelf (Q=1). Reset = remove item (Q=0). Both pressed = forbidden state (chaos).
Like a metronome or heartbeat — provides timing rhythm that synchronizes when state changes can occur in sequential circuits.
Like water flowing through a pipe — there's a delay between input change and output change due to physical constraints.
Like reducing a recipe — remove redundant steps/ingredients without changing the final dish (output).
